title: Expressing support for the recognition of "Detransition Awareness Day".
plain_summary: This bill wants to create a special day called "Detransition Awareness Day" to support people who changed their gender but later decided to change back. The bill says these people need better mental health help and that doctors should do a better job explaining the risks before someone gets gender treatments. It also wants to change laws to help people who say they were hurt by these medical treatments. The goal is to make sure people get all the information they need before making big decisions about changing their gender.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses support for recognizing a day called Detransition Awareness Day.

sections.title: Background Findings
sections.text: The House of Representatives finds that many people who have had medical treatments to change their physical sex characteristics later regret these treatments. The number of people seeking to undo these changes has grown a lot, showing that we need better understanding and support. Many people who had these medical treatments face permanent physical changes and health problems, including damage to their private parts, problems with sexual function, inability to have children, bone disease, and mental distress. There is an important need for complete, non-invasive, and science-based care for people who feel uncomfortable with their sex that focuses on mental health support instead of only physical treatments. People who had sex-changing medical treatments and have gone back to living as their birth sex often face punishment and hateful words for publicly saying they regret their treatments. They also face barriers to getting proper medical, mental health, and social support after choosing to detransition (go back to living as their birth sex), including lack of insurance coverage for treatments to fix or repair damage.

sections.title: House of Representatives Actions
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports recognizing Detransition Awareness Day to acknowledge the experiences of people who have detransitioned or are in the process of detransitioning and have returned to living as their birth sex. This day would help the public and lawmakers understand the challenges these people face. The House urges the creation of policies that make sure complete mental health services are available for people who feel uncomfortable with their sex. It also supports requiring informed consent processes that fully explain the risks of sex-changing medical treatments, including the possibility of regret and the permanent nature of these treatments. The House calls for laws that extend the time limit for filing claims related to medical malpractice or negligence in providing sex-changing treatments, recognizing that harm is often realized later. It also wants to remove any existing limits on money damages for people harmed by these treatments to make sure they get adequate payment for both physical and mental injuries. The House asks the Department of Health and Human Services to review current research about care for people struggling with discomfort about their sex, create new guidelines as needed to promote ethical medical standards that do not involve physically invasive practices that try to change a person's sex characteristics, and revise guidelines to better protect patient rights and health. The House praises people who have detransitioned or are detransitioning and have returned to living as their birth sex for their courage in sharing their stories and working toward better care and understanding for those in similar situations. The House encourages all states to adopt similar resolutions and programs to support these people and to make sure all medical treatments follow the highest ethical standards.
